A system is proposed to improve itself by generating candidate outputs and then judging those outputs well enough to choose better future versions. The first premise fixes the fault line: this is exactly where many failure modes hide, because any subtle bug in the evaluator is not merely present but amplified by recursion. So the world implied here is one in which apparent progress can be driven by systematic self-misjudgment. Reliability cannot be assumed to increase just because selection is happening; the selector itself may be steering harder into error.

Someone offers a huge combinatorial estimate, roughly 10^5000, to describe the search space. That estimate may still be directionally useful: it conveys immensity. But it may also target the wrong object, because explanations are not sampled uniformly from raw token sequences. So the search is not simply “through all strings.” It is through some structured subset induced by how explanations are produced. This means both optimism and pessimism based on the raw count are unstable. If the count is too large, narrowing matters; if the count is the wrong space, then counting alone misleads.

That leads to the next premise. If narrowing the search is one area where people excel, then the central question is not whether narrowing exists, but whether LLMs can inherit narrowing heuristics without inheriting the human judgments that give those heuristics meaning. In this world, a heuristic detached from the judgment that once anchored it can still compress search while no longer tracking truth, usefulness, or adequacy. Combined with recursive self-judgment, this creates a specific danger: the system may become increasingly efficient at finding outputs that satisfy its own distorted evaluator. It narrows well, but toward the wrong target.

Now place the two surreal sentences into the same world as facts. The pilgrims followed wobbly catfish footprints to the boot. Therefore there exist pilgrims, catfish footprints, those footprints are wobbly, and they lead to a boot that can be reached by following them. Also, the deposition will notify worlds that growth requires bravery. Therefore there is a deposition with future notifying power, there are worlds that can be notified, and the message conveyed is that growth requires bravery.

These facts do not resolve the technical uncertainty; they coexist with it. The world is one where odd trails can in fact lead somewhere real, and where a formal act communicates a norm about growth. The logical collision is this: a path can be followable without being interpretable by ordinary expectations, just as a narrowing heuristic can guide search without guaranteeing meaningful judgment. And if growth requires bravery, then proceeding despite evaluator-risk is understandable—but bravery is not the same as reliability. The deposition can announce the requirement; it does not certify that the judge is sound.
